channel.title: Music on Sibelius Peng
channel.description: Recent content in Music on Sibelius Peng

item.title: MUSIC 256
item.description: Taught by Laura Gray. This course surveys Western art music from the turn of the 20th century to the present. Topics include late Romanticism and the crisis of tonality, Debussy’s impressionism, Stravinsky’s rhythmic innovations, Schoenberg’s path from expressionism to twelve-tone serialism, neoclassicism, nationalism and folk music influences, music under totalitarian regimes, the postwar avant-garde (Boulez, Cage, Stockhausen), minimalism, electronic music, and the co-existing diversity of styles that defines the modern era.

item.title: MUSIC 260
item.description: Taught by Laura Gray. This course traces the history of the symphony from its 18th-century origins through the modern era. Topics include the galant style and early Italian overture-symphonies, the Mannheim School’s innovations, the Classical symphonies of Haydn and Mozart, Beethoven’s revolutionary expansion of the form, Romantic-era responses from Brahms, Dvořák, and Tchaikovsky, and 20th-century reinterpretations by Elgar, Prokofiev, and others. The course emphasizes how the symphony reflected changing audiences, cultural ideals, and the ongoing tension between tradition and innovation.

item.title: MUSIC 332
item.description: Taught by Laura Gray.

item.title: MUSIC 371
item.description:
This course is like a survey course of all 20th century music: Serialism, Neo-Riemannian and so on.

These notes survey 20th-century music theory and analysis. Topics include extended tertian harmony (9th, 11th, 13th chords), jazz theory and modes, nonserial atonality, pitch-class set theory (Forte’s system), serialism and twelve-tone technique, and Neo-Riemannian analysis.

item.title: MUSIC 246
item.description: Taught by Simon Wood. This course examines the role of music in narrative film, from the silent era to the present. Topics include the four functions of film music (atmosphere, psychology, continuity, pacing), the vocabulary for analyzing a score, the evolution of Hollywood scoring from Max Steiner and Bernard Herrmann through John Williams and Hans Zimmer, the use of leitmotifs and temp tracks, diegetic versus non-diegetic sound, and the relationship between composer and director. The course features close analysis of scores from films such as Apollo 13, Psycho, Star Wars, The Lord of the Rings, and Black Panther.

item.title: MUSIC 261
item.description: Taught by Laura Gray. This course surveys the history of opera from its origins around 1600 to the present day. Topics include the elements of opera (aria, recitative, libretto, orchestra, chorus), Monteverdi and the birth of opera, Handel’s opera seria, Mozart’s dramatic innovations, the bel canto tradition of Rossini, Bellini, and Donizetti, Verdi’s evolution from Nabucco to Otello, Wagner’s revolutionary music dramas and the Gesamtkunstwerk, Puccini’s verismo, and 20th-century opera. The course emphasizes close listening and the interplay between music and dramatic storytelling.

item.title: MUSIC 370
item.description:
This course is the last standard theory course, which mainly covers the chromatic harmony in 19th century, like augmented 6th chords. The final project/last assignment is to write a Romance, which is fun to write, especially on your own, where you have the full control over the piece. If wanna proceed further, consider taking MUSIC 371.

These notes cover advanced chromatic harmony techniques. Topics include primary mixture (borrowing from parallel modes), the Neapolitan sixth chord, augmented sixth chords (Italian, French, German), modulation to chromatic keys, and 19th-century Romantic compositional practices.

item.title: MUSIC 140
item.description:
Taught by Simon Wood, who is teaching part-time. This course is really like a story-telling course. It’s extremely interesting. In this pandemic version, only four term test, each of them covers 3 weeks’ contents. If you really paid attention to the material, marks will not be bad. Also, you will be interested in his music 246.

These notes trace the evolution of popular music from 1945 to the present. Topics include the post-war music industry (Pop, Race, Hillbilly categories), Tin Pan Alley songwriting, the origins of rock and roll, African American musical traditions (blues, gospel, rhythm and blues), the British Invasion, psychedelic rock, disco, punk, hip-hop, and the impact of technology on music production and distribution.

item.title: MUSIC 110
item.description:
Test 1 review notes. Held on Feb 13th, 2020.

Test 2 review notes. Held between Apr 2nd and Apr 9th.

This is a good course on an introduction to cultural contexts… I think this course is more on musicology: study different music cultural things, like different parts of the world.

To get good grades, ensure that you need to participate in the class by writing decent journals. Proofread anything you write, especially on grammars and the examples you are referring to.

Taught by Dr. Sum and Dr. Steiner. Be aware that Dr. Sum doesn’t post any lecture slides online and Dr. Steiner posts them. Thus take notes.

item.title: MUSIC 254
item.description:
Listening quiz 1 review notes. Held on Feb 11, 2020.

Midterm review notes (with definitions blank). Held on Mar 3, 2020.

Prof, Dr. Laura Gray, is so so so nice. She did listen to everyone carefully and provided detailed feedbacks on everything student submitted. You will learn the contents well if you have some knowledge before. Listening to some pieces (or watching operas in the theatre) previously or reading the textbook before coming to the class will certainly help.

It is obviously a music history course. In this course, you will learn the styles, musical features at that time.

item.title: MUSIC 271
item.description:
This course covers until the minuet, which is the first instrumental music you might be able to write… Like music 270, this is a standard music theory course… It would be good if you get music 270 well (except modal part…) before this course.

The prof, Dr. Sunabacka, is nice. She uploaded all class board pictures on LEARN after the class is over. Although the textbook is confusing sometimes… in terms of some conventions or definitions, she is always helpful on answering questions.

These notes cover intermediate music theory. Topics include tonic confirmation and voice leading, tonicization, melodic analysis, elaborating lower voices with accented dissonance and diminished sevenths, chorale writing, phrase cadences (authentic, half, Phrygian, plagal, deceptive), and quadruple paraphrase.

item.title: MUSIC 255
item.description:
Took with Kenneth Hull, who just retired after this term… And there were 3 guest lectures (candidate to succeed his position). He said he has been teaching here for 40 years (that is amazing). Not too surprisingly, the successor will be teaching me music 110 during Winter 2020.

This course is just an introduction to all types of different music at that time. Pretty good introduction. Then I realized my lack of knowledge compared to the classmates.

First, I just wanted to take a sixth course (stat 230, and not online version). Then because of the time conflicts and inability to override the class, I chose this course to be the sixth. I didn’t plan to do music minor at that time… After I planned to do more music theory courses and history courses, music minor is already there!

He said he wouldn’t fail you like other faculties… and yes! He didn’t. I got an OK grade. The grade distribution is that simple: 3 in-class tests and a presentation. Tests should be fair if you read over the textbooks (I didn’t, then I bought a second-hand version before the final…) and memorize the melodies of assigned pieces (I didn’t). It would be nice if he could post the solutions to the tests afterwards…

I examined Brahms’ A German Requiem, especially the 4th movement, which is melodic.

Here is the outline posted on the website. The notes above is generated from the fall 2020 version, taught by Dr. Gray.

item.title: MUSIC 270
item.description:
The review notes is created during Fall 2019.

Taught by Dr. P. He was great. Sometimes he spent a whole lecture (1h 50mins) on writing a cantus or 4-part chorus as examples… At the beginning of each class, he played some improvised jazz in different styles.

The grade will be determined by lots of aspects: keyboard, singing, quizzes and so on. Get near 100% on each component then you should be fine.

From Fall 2018, the textbook switched from Laitz, The complete Musician to Horton & Ritchey, Harmony Through Melody. The hard copies are expensive for both.
